mappings.rationaleAI: Higher cognitive processes including reasoning, concept formation, and problem-solving (G.1), the role of inner speech and language-of-thought hypotheses (G.2), and theories of meaning in cognition (G.3).
All mental phenomena including thought, perception, and memory relevant to diverse theories of human learning (A), and conscious and unconscious processes underlying higher thought (G).
Transmission of knowledge through formal and informal processes, covering learning theories and teaching methods (A), and transfer of training from educational to applied contexts (A.3).
Encoding, storage, and retrieval of information covering retention and forgetting (E), with amnesia, confabulation, and memory disorders constituting abnormalities of memory (F).

Acquisition of knowledge spanning non-associative, associative, and complex forms as modern learning theories (A.1), with habituation and conditioning as major issues (A.2), and motor (B), perceptual (C), and conceptual (D) learning as distinct domains.
Full range of human responses shaped by psychological and cultural factors, encompassing learning theories (A) and psychomotor behavior (B).
Ability to read and write as a form of perceptual learning (C) and the role of literacy in the relationship between language and higher thought processes (G.2).
Transmission of meaning through coded messages, relevant to the role of language in higher thought processes (G.2) and the study of meaning in communication (G.3).

Structured system of grammar and vocabulary serving as the primary medium for conceptual thought, directly addressing the role of language in higher thought processes (G.2) and semantic meaning (G.3).
Cognitive capacity for deductive, inductive, and analogical reasoning, constituting a core dimension of the higher thought processes (G.1).
Study of knowledge including belief, truth, and justification, relevant to the psychology of higher thought processes (G.1) and theories of meaning (G.3).
Scientific study of cognition, learning, and behavior covering learning theories (A), memory processes (E), and higher cognitive functions (G).
Psychoanalytic theory as a modern learning theory involving unconscious processes (A.1), and repression and free association as mechanisms relevant to abnormalities of memory (F).
Development of abstract and hypothetical reasoning during adolescence as conceptual learning and concept formation (D), and metacognition as a higher thought process (G.1).
Progressive cognitive, physical, and social maturation involving learning theories (A), motor skill development (B), perceptual learning (C), and concept formation (D).
Awareness of facts, familiarity, and practical skill, with epistemological analysis of justified belief relevant to higher thought processes (G.1) and meaning (G.3).
Correspondence, coherence, and pragmatic theories of truth directly address the problem of meaning in cognitive processes (G.3).
Transmission of knowledge through memorization and performance, relying on specialized memory retention techniques relevant to memory and retention of learned content (E).
Contemplative practice training sustained attention and metacognitive monitoring, relevant to perceptual learning (C) and memory enhancement through focused awareness (E).
